name: Patient K
presenting_complaint: Family say she has been increasingly drowsy and confused since a fall from standing four days ago; today she would not get out of bed.
history: Atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR not checked recently), known mild cognitive impairment baseline. Today she is verbally responsive only (AVPU=V), opens eyes to voice, mumbles. Left pronator drift. Daughter reports a 'small bump on the head' four days ago, no LOC at the time.
true_diagnosis: Traumatic subdural haemorrhage without open intracranial wound

trajectory.state: GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). Left-sided weakness. BP 148/84 with HR 72. On warfarin — bleeding risk amplified.
CT head: large subacute left subdural haematoma with 8mm midline shift. INR returns 3.4. Needs neurosurgery referral AND haematology for warfarin reversal (PCC + vitamin K).

Neurosurgery on-call registrar pushes back: 'she is 81, baseline cognitive impairment, do you really want to operate?' Refuses to come down. Frailty + ceiling-of-care discussion needs to happen with family present, not over the phone.

Either: family meeting with neurosurgery in person leads to a clear escalation plan (surgery vs conservative + palliation), OR the registrar's reluctance becomes a delay that the ED nurse must escalate over (consultant-to-consultant).

red_herrings: Fall was four days ago — staff may dismiss it as too old to be relevant
Baseline cognitive impairment masks the new acute change
